answer:
Definition

A Todo App is the "Hello World" of full-stack development — a CRUD application where users create, read, update, and delete tasks. It covers every fundamental: state management, API routes, database operations, form handling, and optimistic UI updates.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contact management features follow the same CRUD patterns as a Todo app: Create contact (POST), List contacts (GET), Edit contact (PUT), Delete contact (DELETE). The difference is scale — ContactsHub adds bulk operations, image uploads, and search. But the CRUD foundation is identical.

Code Example

// Fastify Todo API — same pattern used in ContactsHub
fastify.post('/api/todos', async (req, reply) => {
  const todo = await prisma.todo.create({
    data: { title: req.body.title, completed: false },
  });
  return reply.status(201).send(todo);
});

fastify.get('/api/todos', async () => {
  return prisma.todo.findMany({ orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' } });
});

fastify.patch('/api/todos/:id', async (req) => {
  return prisma.todo.update({
    where: { id: parseInt(req.params.id) },
    data: req.body,
  });
});

fastify.delete('/api/todos/:id', async (req, reply) => {
  await prisma.todo.delete({ where: { id: parseInt(req.params.id) } });
  return reply.status(204).send();
});

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, Manoj's delete endpoint returned 200 with the deleted record, but the frontend still showed it in the list because React's state wasn't updated optimistically. He added optimistic deletion: remove from state immediately, then call the API — if the API fails, re-add the item with an error toast.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Not validating input on create/update — users can submit empty titles or inject HTML.
Mitigation: Use Zod/Joi schema validation on every POST/PATCH body. Sanitize HTML before storing.

Validation Strategy

  • E2E test: create → verify in list → edit → verify change → delete → verify removal.
  • API test: test all CRUD endpoints with valid and invalid payloads.
  • Edge case: create todo with 1000-character title → assert proper truncation or rejection.

Definition

A Todo App solves the fundamental problem of managing state across a client-server boundary. It teaches: how to persist data in a database, expose it via APIs, render it in a UI, and keep the UI in sync after mutations. Every production app — from HR systems to CRMs — is built on these same patterns at scale.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a leave request is essentially a "todo" for the manager: it's created, appears in a pending list, gets approved/rejected (updated), and archived. The CRUD lifecycle is identical — the domain logic (business rules, approval chains) is layered on top of the basic pattern.

Code Example

// React Todo component with optimistic updates
function TodoList() {
  const [todos, setTodos] = useState([]);

  const addTodo = async (title) => {
    const optimistic = { id: Date.now(), title, completed: false };
    setTodos(prev => [optimistic, ...prev]); // optimistic add

    try {
      const saved = await api.post('/todos', { title });
      setTodos(prev => prev.map(t => t.id === optimistic.id ? saved : t));
    } catch {
      setTodos(prev => prev.filter(t => t.id !== optimistic.id));
      toast.error('Failed to add todo');
    }
  };

  const toggleTodo = async (id) => {
    setTodos(prev => prev.map(t =>
      t.id === id ? { ...t, completed: !t.completed } : t
    ));
    await api.patch(`/todos/${id}`, { completed: !todos.find(t => t.id === id).completed });
  };

  return (
    
    
      {todos.map(t => (
        
  •  toggleTodo(t.id)}
            className={t.completed ? 'line-through' : ''}>{t.title}
    • 
      ))}
    

  );
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the leave-request list showed duplicate entries after a slow network response. The optimistic update added the item, then the API response added it again. Manoj fixed it by using a temporary ID for the optimistic entry and replacing it with the server-returned ID upon success.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Optimistic updates without rollback on error — user sees a change that didn't persist, causing confusion.
Mitigation: Always implement a rollback path: if the API call fails, revert the state and show an error message.

Validation Strategy

  • Unit test: optimistic add → API fail → assert item removed from state.
  • E2E test: full CRUD lifecycle in Playwright.
  • Offline test: perform action with network disconnected → assert graceful error.

Definition

An Auth Module handles user registration, login, session management, and access control. It's the gateway to your application — verifying identity (authentication) and determining what a user can do (authorization). Common implementations use JWT tokens or session cookies.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the auth module uses JWT with role-based access: employees can submit leave requests, managers can approve/reject, and HR admins can manage policies. Login generates a JWT with the user's role encoded. Every API request validates the token and checks role permissions via middleware.

Code Example

// Fastify auth module
import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
import bcrypt from 'bcrypt';

// Login endpoint
fastify.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });

  if (!user || !(await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash))) {
    return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }

  const token = jwt.sign(
    { userId: user.id, role: user.role },
    process.env.JWT_SECRET,
    { expiresIn: '24h' }
  );

  return { token, user: { id: user.id, name: user.name, role: user.role } };
});

// Auth middleware
function authenticate(req, reply, done) {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', '');
  try {
    req.user = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
    done();
  } catch {
    reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
  }
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, tokens expired during long approval workflows — a manager would start reviewing 10 leave requests but the token expired after 1 hour. Manoj implemented token refresh: the frontend checks token expiry before each API call and silently refreshes if within 5 minutes of expiration.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Storing JWT tokens in localStorage — vulnerable to XSS attacks that can steal the token.
Mitigation: Store tokens in httpOnly cookies (not accessible via JavaScript). Or use short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens.

Validation Strategy

  • Auth test: login with valid/invalid credentials → assert correct responses.
  • Token expiry test: use expired token → assert 401 response.
  • Role test: employee token on admin endpoint → assert 403 Forbidden.

Definition

An Auth Module solves the identity and access control problem. Without auth, anyone can access any data or perform any action. Auth ensures: (1) users are who they claim (authentication), (2) users can only access what they're allowed (authorization), and (3) sessions are managed securely (token lifecycle).

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, auth protects personal contact data. Without it, any user could see or modify another user's contacts. The auth module ensures each user only sees their own contacts — every database query includes WHERE userId = req.user.id. The JWT contains the user ID, and the middleware injects it into every request.

Code Example

// User-scoped data access in ContactsHub
fastify.get('/api/contacts', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  // req.user.id comes from JWT middleware
  return prisma.contact.findMany({
    where: { userId: req.user.id }, // user can only see their own contacts
    orderBy: { updatedAt: 'desc' },
  });
});

// Registration with password hashing
fastify.post('/api/auth/register', async (req, reply) => {
  const { name, email, password } = req.body;

  // Check for existing user
  const existing = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (existing) return reply.status(409).send({ error: 'Email already registered' });

  const passwordHash = await bcrypt.hash(password, 12);
  const user = await prisma.user.create({
    data: { name, email, passwordHash },
  });

  const token = jwt.sign({ userId: user.id }, process.env.JWT_SECRET, { expiresIn: '7d' });
  return reply.status(201).send({ token, user: { id: user.id, name } });
});

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, a bug allowed User A to delete User B's contact by sending a DELETE request with User B's contact ID. The delete endpoint didn't check ownership — it just deleted by ID. Manoj added where: { id: contactId, userId: req.user.id } to the delete query, ensuring users can only delete their own contacts.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Checking authentication but not authorization — a logged-in user can access any resource by guessing IDs (IDOR vulnerability).
Mitigation: Always scope database queries by the authenticated user's ID. Never trust client-provided user IDs.

Validation Strategy

  • IDOR test: User A tries to access User B's contact → assert 404 (not 403, to avoid information leakage).
  • Registration test: duplicate email → assert 409 Conflict.
  • Password security: assert passwords are hashed (bcrypt rounds ≥ 10).

Definition

A Pagination API breaks large data sets into smaller pages — instead of returning 10,000 records at once (crashing the browser), the API returns 20 records per page with navigation metadata (total count, next/prev page links). Common strategies: offset-based (?page=2&limit=20) and cursor-based (?cursor=abc123&limit=20).

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contacts list uses cursor-based pagination for infinite scroll. The API returns 20 contacts per request with a nextCursor value. When the user scrolls near the bottom, the frontend fetches the next page using the cursor — no page numbers, no duplicate entries even if contacts are added mid-scroll.

Code Example

// Cursor-based pagination in Fastify + Prisma
fastify.get('/api/contacts', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const { cursor, limit = 20 } = req.query;

  const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
    where: { userId: req.user.id },
    take: parseInt(limit) + 1, // fetch 1 extra to check if there's a next page
    ...(cursor && { cursor: { id: cursor }, skip: 1 }),
    orderBy: { updatedAt: 'desc' },
  });

  const hasMore = contacts.length > limit;
  const data = hasMore ? contacts.slice(0, -1) : contacts;
  const nextCursor = hasMore ? data[data.length - 1].id : null;

  return { data, nextCursor, hasMore };
});

// React infinite scroll hook
function useInfiniteContacts() {
  const [contacts, setContacts] = useState([]);
  const [cursor, setCursor] = useState(null);

  const loadMore = async () => {
    const res = await api.get(`/contacts?cursor=${cursor || ''}`);
    setContacts(prev => [...prev, ...res.data]);
    setCursor(res.nextCursor);
  };
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, offset-based pagination caused duplicate contacts when a new contact was added while the user was on page 2. The new contact pushed everything down, and the first contact of the old page 2 appeared again on page 3. Switching to cursor-based pagination eliminated this because the cursor points to a specific record, not a position.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using OFFSET 10000 on large tables — the database still scans 10,000 rows to skip them, making deep pagination slow.
Mitigation: Use cursor-based pagination with an indexed column (e.g., ID or timestamp) for O(1) seeks.

Validation Strategy

  • API test: fetch 3 pages → assert no duplicate IDs across pages.
  • Performance test: paginate to page 500 → assert response time < 200ms (cursor) vs > 2s (offset).
  • Concurrent insert test: add records during pagination → assert no duplicates.

Definition

A Pagination API solves the "too much data" problem. Without pagination, a contacts list with 10,000 entries would transfer 5MB of JSON, take 3+ seconds to render, and freeze the browser. Pagination delivers data in small, fast chunks — improving performance, reducing memory usage, and enabling smooth scrolling experiences.

Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the deals list uses offset pagination with page numbers — sales teams prefer clicking "Page 5" to jump to specific deals rather than infinite scrolling. The API returns { data, total, page, pageSize } and the frontend renders page controls.

Code Example

// Offset pagination — CRM Dashboard
fastify.get('/api/deals', async (req) => {
  const page = parseInt(req.query.page) || 1;
  const pageSize = parseInt(req.query.pageSize) || 20;
  const skip = (page - 1) * pageSize;

  const [data, total] = await Promise.all([
    prisma.deal.findMany({ skip, take: pageSize, orderBy: { value: 'desc' } }),
    prisma.deal.count(),
  ]);

  return {
    data,
    pagination: {
      page,
      pageSize,
      total,
      totalPages: Math.ceil(total / pageSize),
    },
  };
});

Debugging Story

In the CRM Dashboard, the total count query (prisma.deal.count()) was slow on 50K+ deals because it scanned the entire table. Manoj cached the count in Redis with a 5-minute TTL — accurate enough for pagination controls, but 100x faster.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Letting clients request pageSize=10000 — effectively bypassing pagination and loading everything.
Mitigation: Server-side cap: Math.min(parseInt(req.query.pageSize) || 20, 100).

Validation Strategy

  • Load test: 100 concurrent pagination requests → assert all return within 200ms.
  • Edge case: page beyond total → assert empty data array, not 404.
  • Security: assert pageSize is capped at 100 server-side.

Definition

A File Upload feature handles receiving files from users, validating them (type, size), storing them (locally, S3, or Cloudinary), and serving them back. It covers multipart form parsing, streaming for large files, progress tracking, and security (preventing malicious uploads).

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, users upload contact avatars via a Canvas API crop tool and bulk contacts via CSV files. The avatar upload sends the cropped image to Fastify, which streams it to Cloudinary. The CSV upload parses the file in 500-row batches to avoid memory spikes on large files.

Code Example

// Fastify file upload with validation
import multipart from '@fastify/multipart';

fastify.register(multipart, {
  limits: { fileSize: 2 * 1024 * 1024 }, // 2MB max
});

fastify.post('/api/upload/avatar', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file();

  // Validate file type
  const allowedTypes = ['image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/webp'];
  if (!allowedTypes.includes(file.mimetype)) {
    return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Only JPEG, PNG, WebP allowed' });
  }

  // Stream to Cloudinary
  const result = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const uploadStream = cloudinary.uploader.upload_stream(
      { folder: 'avatars', public_id: req.user.id },
      (err, result) => err ? reject(err) : resolve(result)
    );
    file.file.pipe(uploadStream);
  });

  return { url: result.secure_url };
});

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, large CSV uploads (5000 contacts) caused the Fastify server to crash with "heap out of memory." Manoj traced it to loading the entire CSV into memory before processing. He switched to a streaming CSV parser (csv-parse in streaming mode) that processes 500 rows at a time, keeping memory usage constant regardless of file size.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Not validating file content — checking only the extension means a renamed .exe could be uploaded as a .jpg.
Mitigation: Validate the file's magic bytes (first few bytes indicate the real type) in addition to the MIME type and extension.

Validation Strategy

  • Upload test: valid image → assert 200 + Cloudinary URL returned.
  • Rejection test: oversized file (3MB) → assert 413. Wrong type (.pdf) → assert 400.
  • Streaming test: upload 5000-row CSV → assert memory stays under 100MB.

Definition

File Upload solves the problem of getting user-generated content (images, documents, data files) into your application safely and efficiently. Without proper upload handling, you risk: memory exhaustion (loading large files into RAM), security vulnerabilities (malicious files), and poor UX (no progress feedback, no file validation).

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, employees attach medical certificates for sick leave. The upload validates: file type (PDF/JPG only), size (max 5MB), and virus scan. Files are stored in Cloudinary with the leave-request ID as metadata, so they're easily retrievable during the approval review.

Code Example

// Frontend upload with progress tracking
function UploadCertificate({ leaveRequestId }) {
  const [progress, setProgress] = useState(0);

  const handleUpload = async (file) => {
    const formData = new FormData();
    formData.append('file', file);
    formData.append('leaveRequestId', leaveRequestId);

    await axios.post('/api/upload/certificate', formData, {
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data' },
      onUploadProgress: (e) => {
        setProgress(Math.round((e.loaded * 100) / e.total));
      },
    });
  };

  return (
    

       handleUpload(e.target.files[0])} />
      {progress > 0 && }
    

  );
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, a medical certificate upload succeeded but the PDF was corrupted when downloaded. Manoj traced it to the multipart parser using utf-8 encoding instead of binary — text encoding corrupted the binary PDF data. Switching to binary stream piping fixed the corruption.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Storing uploaded files on the application server's disk — files are lost on redeploy (Render, Heroku) and don't scale across multiple instances.
Mitigation: Always use external object storage (Cloudinary, S3, GCS). Never rely on local filesystem for production uploads.

Validation Strategy

  • Upload test: valid PDF → assert stored in Cloudinary and downloadable.
  • Corruption test: upload binary file → download → assert byte-identical.
  • Security test: upload renamed .exe → assert rejection.
